methodology.split_rationale: ML-style train/validation split — training tests given to AI during implementation, validation tests hidden and run only for evaluation

context.notes: maina context via MCP getContext — retrieved benchmark types, constitution, episodic memory

suggestTests.notes: MCP suggestTests returned generic stubs — spec not in plan.md format

verify.notes: First run: 2 findings (unused variable, formatting). Fixed both. Second run: 0 findings.

review.notes: Two-stage review (spec compliance + code quality) both passed. 0 actionable findings.

self_review.notes: Found and fixed Gmail domain-specific dead code

shared_failure.test: isURL validation — host > invalid IPv4 host
shared_failure.description: Both implementations accept http://999.999.999.999 as valid — URL validator does not call isIP() to validate IP address hosts
shared_failure.root_cause: Cross-function integration gap: isIP exists but is not used within isURL's host validation path

learnings: Train/validation split reveals bugs invisible to both pipelines — the hidden test set is essential for honest evaluation
Both AI pipelines produce near-identical quality (94/95 = 98.9%) on tier 3 complexity
The shared failure (IP host validation in URLs) is a cross-function integration bug — spec says 'IP addresses are validated according to isIP rules' but neither pipeline enforced this
Maina's verify caught 2 real issues (unused var, formatting) and fixed them; review ran two-stage (spec+quality) with 0 actionable findings
Spec Kit was 31% faster (141s vs 204s) because it skips verify+review overhead
Maina produced leaner code (403 LOC vs 504 LOC) — verify/review may encourage tighter implementations
Self-review (Spec Kit) caught 1 real issue (Gmail dead code); maina verify caught 3 issues but missed the critical IP host bug
Validation test set justifies its existence: without it, both pipelines would show 100% and appear identical
For tier 3+ complexity, the verification pipeline needs to be tuned to escalate cross-function integration checks from info to warning level
The spec deliberately mentioned 'validated according to isIP rules' — a spec-compliance checker should have flagged this gap
